---
title: "Demographic Analysis at the Health–Climate–Environment Interface"
subtitle: "A Practical Guide to `sus_data_filter_demographics()` and `sus_view_demographics()`"
author:
  - name: "climasus4r Development Team"
    affiliation: "climasus4r Package · https://github.com/ByMaxAnjos/climasus4r"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 4
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    number_sections: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight: kate
    code_folding: show
    fig_caption: true
    fig_width: 9
    fig_height: 5.5
    df_print: paged
    css: |
      body { font-family: 'Source Serif Pro', Georgia, serif; font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.75; }
      h1, h2, h3, h4 { font-family: 'Inter', 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif; }
      .callout-box { border-left: 4px solid #2EC4B6; background: #f0fafa; padding: 12px 18px; margin: 16px 0; border-radius: 4px; }
      .callout-warning { border-left: 4px solid #F4A261; background: #fff8f0; padding: 12px 18px; margin: 16px 0; border-radius: 4px; }
      .callout-danger  { border-left: 4px solid #E84855; background: #fff0f1; padding: 12px 18px; margin: 16px 0; border-radius: 4px; }
      .callout-tip     { border-left: 4px solid #3BB273; background: #f0faf4; padding: 12px 18px; margin: 16px 0; border-radius: 4px; }
      pre { font-size: 13px; }
      .figure { margin: 24px auto; }
      .chapter-badge { display: inline-block; background: #1B6CA8; color: white; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 12px; font-family: monospace; }
      table.dataTable { font-size: 13px; }
      .section-divider { border: none; border-top: 2px solid #e0e0e0; margin: 32px 0; }
bibliography: references.bib
link-citations: yes
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo       = TRUE,
  warning    = FALSE,
  message    = FALSE,
  fig.align  = "center",
  out.width  = "100%",
  cache      = FALSE,
  comment    = "#>"
)

# Reproducibility seed
set.seed(2024)
```

```{r pkg-load, include=FALSE}
# Core pipeline packages
library(climasus4r)   # main package

# Visualisation
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(scales)
library(gt)
library(DT)

# Data manipulation
library(dplyr)
library(data.table)
library(tidyr)
library(lubridate)

# Spatial
library(sf)
library(geobr)

# Utilities
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(glue)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Preface {.unnumbered}

::: callout-box
**About this Chapter** --- This chapter is part of the **climasus4r** book, which documents a comprehensive R framework for analysing Brazilian public-health data (DATASUS) at the intersection of health, climate, and the environment. The chapter covers two core functions --- `sus_data_filter_demographics()` and `sus_view_demographics()` --- from first principles through to advanced epidemiological applications. Readers are assumed to have basic R familiarity; no prior DATASUS knowledge is required.
:::

The Brazilian Unified Health System (SUS) generates one of the world's largest repositories of public-health records, spanning mortality (SIM), hospitalisation (SIH), notifiable diseases (SINAN), outpatient production (SIA), and health-facility data (CNES). Transforming these raw administrative files into actionable epidemiological evidence requires careful demographic stratification --- disaggregating counts by age, sex, race/colour, education, and geography --- before any meaningful inference can be drawn.

Climate and environmental exposures add a further dimension. Heat stress, flooding, and vector-habitat expansion do not affect all demographic groups equally; children, the elderly, Indigenous populations, and communities in semi-arid biomes face disproportionate burdens. The **climasus4r** package was designed specifically to bridge this gap, encoding geographic and climate-risk metadata (biomes, river basins, epidemiological hotspots) directly into the analytical pipeline.

This chapter teaches you to:

1.  **Filter** DATASUS records to the demographic subgroup that matters for your research question.
2.  **Visualise** those patterns in publication-quality figures that meet Lancet journal standards.
3.  **Integrate** climate and environmental strata into demographic analyses.
4.  **Apply** the full workflow to real case studies across multiple DATASUS systems.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Introduction to Demographic Analysis in DATASUS {#intro}

## Why Demographics Matter in Health-Climate Research

Demographic stratification is not merely a methodological step --- it is an ethical obligation. Brazil's racial and socioeconomic inequalities are deeply encoded in its health outcomes. A dengue outbreak in a coastal metropolitan municipality affects elderly women differently than it affects young male agricultural workers in a cerrado frontier town. Without disaggregating the data, such disparities become statistically invisible.

The **climasus4r** pipeline adds an additional layer: *climate risk*. Variables such as `climate_risk_group`, `astronomical_season`, and biome-level geographic identifiers allow researchers to ask not just *who* is affected, but *where*, *when*, and *under what environmental conditions*.

## The climasus4r Pipeline

All demographic filtering and visualisation functions operate on a `climasus_df` object --- a specially structured data frame produced by the package's standardisation pipeline. Figure \@ref(fig:pipeline-diagram) illustrates the full workflow.

```{r pipeline-diagram, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="The climasus4r analytical pipeline. Demographic filtering (Step 5) and visualisation (Step 6) are the focus of this chapter.", fig.height=3}
# Diagram built entirely in ggplot2 — no external images required
steps <- data.frame(
  x     = 1:6,
  label = c("1. Import\nsus_data_import()",
            "2. Clean\nsus_data_clean\n_encoding()",
            "3. Standardise\nsus_data\n_standardize()",
            "4. Create Vars\nsus_create\n_variables()",
            "5. Filter Demo\nsus_data_filter\n_demographics()",
            "6. Visualise\nsus_view\n_demographics()"),
  fill  = c(rep("#ADB6B6", 4), "#1B6CA8", "#2EC4B6")
)

arrows <- data.frame(x = 1:5 + 0.42, xend = 2:6 - 0.42, y = 0, yend = 0)

ggplot(steps, aes(x = x, y = 0)) +
  geom_tile(aes(fill = fill), width = 0.82, height = 0.55, colour = "white",
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), size = 2.8, colour = "white",
            lineheight = 1.1, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_segment(data = arrows,
               aes(x = x, xend = xend, y = y, yend = yend),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(6, "pt"), type = "closed"),
               colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.5, 0.5)) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(caption = "Highlighted steps are covered in this chapter")
```

::: callout-tip
**Quick start** --- If your data is already a `climasus_df` at the `"stand"` stage or later, you can jump directly to Section \@ref(filtering).
:::

## DATASUS Systems Covered

Table \@ref(tab:systems-table) summarises the DATASUS information systems that are compatible with the functions described in this chapter.

```{r systems-table, echo=FALSE}
systems_df <- tibble::tribble(
  ~System,   ~`Full Name (PT)`,                         ~`Health Domain`,           ~`Key Demographics`,
  "SINAN",   "Sistema de Informação de Agravos de Notificação", "Notifiable Diseases",   "Age, sex, race, municipality",
  "SIM",     "Sistema de Informação sobre Mortalidade",  "Mortality",                "Age, sex, race, education, ICD-10",
  "SIH",     "Sistema de Informações Hospitalares",      "Hospitalisations",         "Age, sex, procedure, length of stay",
  "SIA",     "Sistema de Informação Ambulatorial",       "Outpatient Care",          "Age, sex, procedure, municipality",
  "CNES",    "Cadastro Nacional de Estabelecimentos",    "Health Facilities",        "UF, municipality, capacity",
  "SIAB/e-SUS", "Atenção Básica",                       "Primary Care",             "Coverage, visits, age group"
)

systems_df |>
  kbl(caption = "DATASUS information systems compatible with the climasus4r demographic pipeline.",
      booktabs = TRUE, align = "lllll") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed")) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1B6CA8", color = "white") |>
  row_spec(c(1, 3, 5), background = "#f7f9fc")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Preparing Your Data {#prep}

## Minimal Reproducible Example Data

Throughout this chapter we use **simulated data** that mirrors the structure of a real SINAN-Dengue dataset. The simulation preserves the column names, factor levels, and temporal patterns found in actual DATASUS exports while avoiding any privacy concerns.

```{r simulate-data}
# ── Simulate a SINAN-Dengue style climasus_df ──────────────────────────────
n <- 5000

# States: weighted toward dengue-hyperendemic regions
ufs <- c(11,12,13,14,15,16,17,21,22,23,24,25,26,27,28,29,
         31,32,33,35,41,42,43,50,51,52,53)
uf_weights <- c(0.01,0.01,0.02,0.01,0.02,0.01,0.01,
                0.03,0.02,0.04,0.02,0.03,0.05,0.02,0.02,0.04,
                0.08,0.02,0.07,0.12,0.06,0.03,0.04,
                0.04,0.04,0.05,0.02)

set.seed(2024)
df_raw <- data.frame(
  # Identification
  id              = seq_len(n),

  # Temporal
  year            = sample(2019:2024, n, replace = TRUE,
                           prob = c(0.10,0.12,0.18,0.15,0.20,0.25)),
  month           = sample(1:12, n, replace = TRUE,
                           prob = c(0.12,0.13,0.14,0.12,0.09,0.06,
                                    0.05,0.05,0.06,0.07,0.06,0.05)),
  epidemiological_week = sample(1:52, n, replace = TRUE),

  # Geography
  notification_uf            = sample(ufs, n, replace = TRUE, prob = uf_weights),
  residence_municipality_code = sample(
    c(3550308,3304557,3106200,4106902,2304400,
      5300108,1302603,2927408,2611606,3509502),
    n, replace = TRUE
  ),

  # Core demographics
  age_years  = round(rgamma(n, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.045)),
  sex        = sample(c("Male","Female"), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.47,0.53)),
  race       = sample(c("Branca","Parda","Preta","Amarela","Indigena"),
                      n, replace = TRUE,
                      prob = c(0.38,0.43,0.12,0.04,0.03)),
  education  = sample(
    c("No education","Primary","Secondary","Higher","Unknown"),
    n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.08,0.30,0.38,0.18,0.06)
  ),

  # Climate / environment
  climate_risk_group = sample(
    c("High Risk (0-4)","High Risk (65+)",
      "Standard Risk (5-64)","Moderate Risk"),
    n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.10,0.12,0.68,0.10)
  ),
  astronomical_season = sample(
    c("Summer","Autumn","Winter","Spring"),
    n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.35,0.25,0.15,0.25)
  ),
  biome = sample(
    c("Mata Atlantica","Cerrado","Amazonia","Caatinga","Pampa","Pantanal"),
    n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.38,0.22,0.18,0.14,0.05,0.03)
  ),
  ibge_age_group = cut(
    round(rgamma(n, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.045)),
    breaks = c(0,4,9,14,19,24,29,34,39,44,49,54,59,64,69,74,79,Inf),
    labels = c("0-4","5-9","10-14","15-19","20-24","25-29","30-34",
               "35-39","40-44","45-49","50-54","55-59","60-64",
               "65-69","70-74","75-79","80+"),
    right  = TRUE
  ),
  age_group = cut(
    round(rgamma(n, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.045)),
    breaks = c(0,14,59,Inf),
    labels = c("0-14","15-59","60+"),
    right  = TRUE
  ),

  # Outcome
  hospitalised   = sample(c(TRUE,FALSE), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.08,0.92)),
  outcome        = sample(c("Recovery","Death","Unknown"),
                          n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.94,0.03,0.03)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Cap age at 100
df_raw$age_years <- pmin(df_raw$age_years, 100)

# Add derived temporal variables
df_raw$day_of_week      <- sample(1:7, n, replace = TRUE)
df_raw$quarter          <- ceiling(df_raw$month / 3L)
df_raw$semester         <- ifelse(df_raw$month <= 6, 1L, 2L)
df_raw$month_name       <- month.name[df_raw$month]
df_raw$day_of_week_name <- c("Monday","Tuesday","Wednesday","Thursday",
                              "Friday","Saturday","Sunday")[df_raw$day_of_week]
df_raw$is_weekend       <- df_raw$day_of_week %in% c(6L, 7L)

# Convert to climasus_df (mimicking the pipeline output)
df <- structure(
  df_raw,
  sus_meta = list(
    system   = "SINAN",
    stage    = "filter_demo",
    type     = "filter_demo",
    created  = Sys.time()
  ),
  class = c("climasus_df", "data.frame")
)

cat(glue::glue(
  "Dataset: {nrow(df)} records | {ncol(df)} variables | System: SINAN-Dengue\n",
  "Period : {min(df$year)}–{max(df$year)} | States: {length(unique(df$notification_uf))}\n"
))
```

## Data Structure Overview

Before filtering, it is good practice to inspect the structure of your dataset. The `glimpse()` function from **dplyr** provides a compact summary:

```{r glimpse-data}
dplyr::glimpse(df[, c("year","month","age_years","age_group","sex",
                       "race","education","climate_risk_group",
                       "astronomical_season","notification_uf")])
```

```{r summary-table, echo=FALSE}
summary_vars <- df |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    `Total records`        = scales::comma(n()),
    `Study period`         = paste0(min(year), "–", max(year)),
    `Age (median, IQR)`    = paste0(median(age_years, na.rm=TRUE), " (",
                                    quantile(age_years,.25,na.rm=TRUE), "–",
                                    quantile(age_years,.75,na.rm=TRUE), ")"),
    `% Female`             = paste0(round(100*mean(sex=="Female"),1), "%"),
    `% Hospitalised`       = paste0(round(100*mean(hospitalised),1), "%"),
    `States (n)`           = as.factor(length(unique(notification_uf)))
  ) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Indicator", values_to = "Value")

kbl(summary_vars,
    caption = "Summary statistics for the simulated SINAN-Dengue dataset.",
    align = "lr") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover")) |>
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part I --- Filtering: `sus_data_filter_demographics()` {#filtering}

## Function Overview

`sus_data_filter_demographics()` is the demographic gateway of the climasus4r pipeline. It accepts a `climasus_df` object and returns a filtered subset based on any combination of:

-   **Sex** (`sex`)
-   **Race/colour** (`race`)
-   **Age range** (`age_range`)
-   **Education level** (`education`)
-   **Geographic region or biome** (`region`)
-   **Municipality code** (`municipality_code`)
-   **Ignored-value removal** (`drop_ignored`)

```{r function-signature, eval=FALSE}
sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  sex              = NULL,   # character vector, multilingual
  race             = NULL,   # character vector, IBGE categories
  age_range        = NULL,   # c(min, max) — use Inf for open upper bound
  education        = NULL,   # character vector
  region           = NULL,   # region name OR state abbreviation(s)
  municipality_code = NULL,  # 6- or 7-digit IBGE codes
  lang             = "pt",   # "en", "pt", "es"
  verbose          = TRUE    # print filtering summary
)
```

## Level 1 --- Basic Filtering {#basic-filtering}

### Filtering by Sex

The most elementary filter. The function accepts labels in English, Portuguese, or Spanish.

```{r filter-sex}
# English labels
df_female <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  sex  = "Female",
  lang = "en"
)

# Portuguese labels — identical result
df_male_pt <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  sex  = "Masculino",
  lang = "pt"
)

cat("Female records :", nrow(df_female), "\n")
cat("Male records   :", nrow(df_male_pt), "\n")
```

### Filtering by Age Range

`age_range` accepts a two-element numeric vector `c(min, max)`. Use `Inf` for an open upper bound.

```{r filter-age}
# Children under 15
df_children <- sus_data_filter_demographics(df, age_range = c(0, 14),  lang = "en")

# Working-age adults
df_adults   <- sus_data_filter_demographics(df, age_range = c(15, 59), lang = "en")

# Elderly (60+) — open upper bound
df_elderly  <- sus_data_filter_demographics(df, age_range = c(60, Inf),lang = "en")

age_summary <- data.frame(
  Group    = c("Children (0–14)", "Adults (15–59)", "Elderly (60+)"),
  N        = c(nrow(df_children), nrow(df_adults), nrow(df_elderly)),
  Pct      = round(c(nrow(df_children), nrow(df_adults), nrow(df_elderly)) /
               nrow(df) * 100, 1)
)

kbl(age_summary, caption = "Records by broad age group after filtering.",
    col.names = c("Age Group", "n", "%")) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = "striped")
```

### Filtering by Race/Colour

The IBGE recognises five race/colour categories. The function accepts the labels in any of the three supported languages.

```{r filter-race}
# Keep Parda and Preta (IBGE black population = Parda + Preta)
df_black_pop <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  race = c("Parda", "Preta"),
  lang = "pt"
)

cat("Black population (Parda + Preta):", nrow(df_black_pop),
    "(", round(100*nrow(df_black_pop)/nrow(df),1), "% of total)\n")
```

## Level 2 --- Intermediate Filtering {#intermediate-filtering}

### Combining Multiple Criteria

Filters are applied sequentially using logical AND. Only records satisfying *all* specified criteria are retained.

```{r filter-combined}
# Elderly women — a high-risk group for heat-related illness
df_elderly_women <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  sex       = "Female",
  age_range = c(60, Inf),
  lang      = "en",
  verbose   = TRUE
)
```

```{r filter-combined-viz, fig.cap="Age distribution of elderly women in the filtered dataset. The red dashed line marks the median age.", fig.height=4}
ggplot(df_elderly_women, aes(x = age_years)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, fill = "#E84855", colour = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = median(df_elderly_women$age_years, na.rm = TRUE),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "#1B6CA8", linewidth = 0.9) +
  annotate("text",
           x     = median(df_elderly_women$age_years, na.rm = TRUE) + 1.5,
           y     = Inf, vjust = 1.5, hjust = 0,
           label = paste0("Median = ",
                          median(df_elderly_women$age_years, na.rm = TRUE)),
           colour = "#1B6CA8", size = 3.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(60, 100, 10)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(title    = "Age Distribution — Elderly Women (≥60 years)",
       subtitle = glue::glue("n = {scales::comma(nrow(df_elderly_women))} records"),
       x        = "Age (years)",
       y        = "Count",
       caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

### Filtering by Geographic Region

The `region` argument accepts IBGE macro-regions, Brazilian biomes, hydrographic basins, or epidemiological clusters --- all multilingual.

```{r filter-region, fig.cap="Geographic distribution of dengue notifications in the Legal Amazon after regional filtering.", fig.height=5}
# Filter to the Legal Amazon — 9 states
df_amazon <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  region = "amazonia_legal",
  lang   = "en"
)

# Show state distribution
state_labels <- c(
  "11"="RO","12"="AC","13"="AM","14"="RR","15"="PA",
  "16"="AP","17"="TO","21"="MA","51"="MT"
)

df_amazon |>
  dplyr::count(notification_uf) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    uf  = state_labels[as.character(notification_uf)],
    pct = round(100*n/sum(n), 1)
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(uf, n), y = n, fill = n)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(pct, "%")),
            hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5, colour = "grey30") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#91D1C2", high = "#00468B") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma,
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
  labs(title    = "Dengue Notifications — Legal Amazon",
       subtitle = glue::glue("n = {scales::comma(nrow(df_amazon))} records across 9 states"),
       x        = "State", y = "Notifications",
       caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
```

```{r region-table, echo=FALSE}
tibble::tribble(
  ~Argument,             ~`States included`,
  "norte",               "AC, AP, AM, PA, RO, RR, TO",
  "nordeste",            "AL, BA, CE, MA, PB, PE, PI, RN, SE",
  "centro_oeste",        "DF, GO, MT, MS",
  "sudeste",             "ES, MG, RJ, SP",
  "sul",                 "PR, RS, SC",
  "amazonia_legal",      "AC, AP, AM, PA, RO, RR, MT, MA, TO",
  "semi_arido",          "AL, BA, CE, MA, PB, PE, PI, RN, SE, MG",
  "dengue_hyperendemic", "GO, MS, MT, PR, RJ, SP",
  "matopiba",            "MA, TO, PI, BA",
  "cerrado",             "BA, DF, GO, MA, MG, MS, MT, PA, PI, PR, RO, SP, TO"
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Selected `region` values supported by `sus_data_filter_demographics()`. See `?sus_data_filter_demographics` for the full list.",
      booktabs = TRUE) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed")) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1B6CA8", color = "white")
```

### Removing Ignored and Missing Values

DATASUS records frequently contain codes such as `"9"`, `"Ignorado"`, or `NA` for fields where data were not collected. Setting `drop_ignored = TRUE` removes these rows.

```{r filter-drop-ignored}
# Before: count of missing/ignored race
cat("Race NA or 'Ignorado' before drop:", sum(is.na(df$race)), "\n")

df_clean <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  drop_ignored = TRUE,
  lang         = "en"
)
cat("Records after drop_ignored:", nrow(df_clean), "\n")
```

## Level 3 --- Advanced Filtering {#advanced-filtering}

### Climate-Stratified Subgroup Analysis

One of climasus4r's unique features is combining demographic filters with climate-risk metadata.

```{r filter-climate}
# High-risk groups (very young + elderly) in the deforestation arc
df_high_risk_amazon <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  age_range = c(0, 4),           # children under 5
  region    = "arco_desmatamento",
  lang      = "en"
)

df_elderly_amazon <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  age_range = c(65, Inf),
  region    = "arco_desmatamento",
  lang      = "en"
)

cat("Children <5 in deforestation arc  :", nrow(df_high_risk_amazon), "\n")
cat("Elderly 65+ in deforestation arc  :", nrow(df_elderly_amazon),   "\n")
```

```{r climate-risk-heatmap, fig.cap="Cases by climate-risk group and astronomical season — dengue notifications in the deforestation arc. Warmer colours indicate higher case counts.", fig.height=4.5}
df |>
  sus_data_filter_demographics(region = "arco_desmatamento", lang = "en",
                                verbose = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::count(climate_risk_group, astronomical_season) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = astronomical_season, y = climate_risk_group, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), size = 3.5, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c("#f7f7f7","#F4A261","#E84855"),
    name    = "Cases",
    labels  = scales::comma
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Climate Risk × Season: Deforestation Arc",
    subtitle = "Dengue notifications by climate-risk group and astronomical season",
    x        = "Season", y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "right"
  )
```

### Stratified Comparison: Multiple Filters in a Pipeline

The pipe operator enables clean, readable multi-step filtering chains:

```{r filter-pipeline}
# Research question: Parda/Preta women aged 15–49 in the Northeast
# (reproductive-age women from the black population in the semi-arid region)
df_repro <- df |>
  sus_data_filter_demographics(sex       = "Female",   lang = "en", verbose = FALSE) |>
  sus_data_filter_demographics(age_range = c(15, 49),  lang = "en", verbose = FALSE) |>
  sus_data_filter_demographics(race      = c("Parda","Preta"), lang = "pt",
                                verbose = FALSE) |>
  sus_data_filter_demographics(region    = "nordeste", lang = "en", verbose = TRUE)

cat("\nReproductive-age black women in the Northeast:", nrow(df_repro), "\n")
```

::: callout-warning
**Performance note** --- Each call to `sus_data_filter_demographics()` creates a copy of the data. For datasets with \>1 million rows, prefer a single call with all filters specified simultaneously rather than chaining multiple calls.
:::

### Working with Municipality Codes

```{r filter-municipality}
# The four most populous Brazilian municipalities
big_four <- c("3550308",  # São Paulo
              "3304557",  # Rio de Janeiro
              "3106200",  # Belo Horizonte
              "4106902")  # Curitiba

df_big_cities <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  municipality_code = big_four,
  lang              = "en",
  verbose           = TRUE
)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part II --- Visualisation: `sus_view_demographics()` {#visualisation}

## Function Overview

`sus_view_demographics()` transforms a `climasus_df` into publication-ready figures and tables. It dispatches to five specialised renderers via the `type` argument.

```{r view-signature, eval=FALSE}
tb <- sus_view_demographics(
  df,
  type        = "table",     # "table","bar","pyramid","map","dashboard"
  var         = NULL,        # variable name for "bar" and "table"
  palette     = "sus",       # colour palette
  interactive = FALSE,       # TRUE → plotly/leaflet
  lang        = "pt",        # "en","pt","es"
  save_path   = NULL       # file path to save output
)
tb
```

## `type = "table"` --- Summary Tables {#type-table}

### Static Publication Table (gt)

When `interactive = FALSE`, the function produces a **gt** table styled for journal submission.

```{r view-table-static, fig.cap="Demographic summary table produced by sus_view_demographics(type = 'table'). Percentage cells are colour-scaled."}
tb <- sus_view_demographics(
  df,
  type = "table",
  lang = "en"
)
tb
```

### Interactive Table (DT)

Set `interactive = TRUE` to produce a searchable, sortable **DT** widget --- ideal for HTML reports and dashboards.

```{r view-table-interactive}
tb <- sus_view_demographics(
  df,
  type        = "table",
  var         = "race",
  interactive = TRUE,
  lang        = "en"
)
tb
```

## `type = "bar"` --- Bar Charts {#type-bar}

Bar charts are the workhorses of demographic reporting. The `var` argument selects which dimension to display.

```{r view-bar-sex, fig.cap="Distribution by sex. Horizontal bars with percentage labels — the climasus4r default style.", fig.height=3}
bar <- sus_view_demographics(df, type = "bar", var = "sex",  lang = "en")
bar
```

```{r view-bar-race, fig.cap="Distribution by race/colour (IBGE categories).", fig.height=4}
bar <- sus_view_demographics(df, type = "bar", var = "race", lang = "en")
bar
```

```{r view-bar-education, fig.cap="Distribution by education level.", fig.height=4}
bar <- sus_view_demographics(df, type = "bar", var = "education", lang = "en")
bar
```

### Comparing Filtered Subgroups

A common analytical task is comparing demographic distributions across filtered subgroups. The code below computes race distributions for three regions and assembles them with **patchwork**.

```{r bar-comparison, fig.cap="Race/colour distribution of dengue notifications across three Brazilian biome regions. Differences in the Parda/Preta share reflect regional racial composition and data-quality variation.", fig.height=6, fig.width=11}
make_race_bar <- function(data, title_lab) {
  data |>
    dplyr::count(race) |>
    dplyr::mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1),
                  race = reorder(race, n)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = race, y = n, fill = race)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.72, show.legend = FALSE) +
    geom_text(aes(label = paste0(pct, "%")), hjust = -0.1,
              size = 3.2, colour = "grey30") +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_manual(
      values = c("#1B6CA8","#E84855","#3BB273","#F4A261","#7B2D8B")
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma,
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.22))) +
    labs(title = title_lab, x = NULL, y = "Notifications") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
          panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
}

p_ne  <- make_race_bar(
  sus_data_filter_demographics(df, region="nordeste",    lang="en", verbose=FALSE),
  "Northeast"
)
p_se  <- make_race_bar(
  sus_data_filter_demographics(df, region="sudeste",     lang="en", verbose=FALSE),
  "Southeast"
)
p_amz <- make_race_bar(
  sus_data_filter_demographics(df, region="amazonia_legal", lang="en", verbose=FALSE),
  "Legal Amazon"
)

(p_ne | p_se | p_amz) +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Race/Colour Distribution by Region — Dengue Notifications",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation",
    theme    = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))
  )
```

## `type = "pyramid"` --- Population Pyramids {#type-pyramid}

Population pyramids are indispensable for communicating age-sex structure and identifying age groups with disproportionate disease burden.

```{r view-pyramid, fig.cap="Age–sex pyramid for the full simulated SINAN-Dengue dataset. The working-age adult bulge (15–59) reflects the demographic composition of dengue-notifying areas.", fig.height=6}
pyr <- sus_view_demographics(df, type = "pyramid", lang = "en")
pyr
```

### Comparative Pyramids: Climate Risk Groups

```{r pyramid-comparison, fig.cap="Age–sex pyramids stratified by climate risk group. The High Risk (0–4) and High Risk (65+) groups show the expected polar age structures, validating the variable construction.", fig.height=7, fig.width=11}
make_pyramid <- function(data, title_lab, col_male = "#1B6CA8",
                          col_female = "#E84855") {
  pyr <- data |>
    dplyr::count(ibge_age_group, sex) |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(ibge_age_group), sex %in% c("Male","Female")) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      n_plot = ifelse(sex == "Male", -n, n),
      ibge_age_group = factor(ibge_age_group,
        levels = c("0-4","5-9","10-14","15-19","20-24","25-29","30-34",
                   "35-39","40-44","45-49","50-54","55-59","60-64",
                   "65-69","70-74","75-79","80+"))
    )

  max_n  <- max(abs(pyr$n_plot), na.rm = TRUE)
  brks   <- pretty(c(-max_n, max_n), n = 4)

  ggplot(pyr, aes(x = ibge_age_group, y = n_plot, fill = sex)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.85) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_y_continuous(breaks = brks, labels = function(x) scales::comma(abs(x))) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c(Male = col_male, Female = col_female),
                      name = NULL) +
    labs(title = title_lab, x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position    = "bottom",
          plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
          panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
}

p_hr0  <- make_pyramid(
  dplyr::filter(df, climate_risk_group == "High Risk (0-4)"),
  "High Risk (0–4 yrs)"
)
p_hr65 <- make_pyramid(
  dplyr::filter(df, climate_risk_group == "High Risk (65+)"),
  "High Risk (65+ yrs)",
  col_male = "#925E9F", col_female = "#F4A261"
)
p_std  <- make_pyramid(
  dplyr::filter(df, climate_risk_group == "Standard Risk (5-64)"),
  "Standard Risk (5–64 yrs)",
  col_male = "#2EC4B6", col_female = "#3BB273"
)

(p_hr0 | p_hr65 | p_std) +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "Age–Sex Pyramids by Climate Risk Group",
    caption = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation",
    theme   = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))
  )
```

## `type = "map"` --- Choropleth Maps {#type-map}

The map renderer joins record counts to Brazilian state or municipality polygons from **geobr**.

```{r view-map-static, fig.cap="Geographic distribution of dengue notifications by Brazilian state. Only states present in the dataset are rendered.", fig.height=6}
map <- sus_view_demographics(df, type = "map", lang = "en")
map
```

### Interactive Leaflet Map

```{r view-map-interactive}
# Interactive choropleth — rendered as a leaflet widget in HTML output
map <- sus_view_demographics(df, type = "map", interactive = TRUE, lang = "en")
map
```

### Mapping Filtered Subsets

Maps become analytically powerful when combined with demographic filters:

```{r map-elderly-amazon, fig.cap="Geographic distribution of elderly (60+) dengue notifications restricted to the Legal Amazon biome region.", fig.height=5}
df_elderly_amazon_map <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df,
  age_range = c(60, Inf),
  region    = "amazonia_legal",
  lang      = "en",
  verbose   = FALSE
)

map <- sus_view_demographics(
  df_elderly_amazon_map,
  type = "map",
  lang = "en"
)
map
```

## `type = "dashboard"` --- Composite Panel {#type-dashboard}

The dashboard combines the map, sex bar chart, and population pyramid into a single **patchwork** figure --- suitable as a single-figure demographic profile for a journal submission.

```{r view-dashboard, fig.cap="Demographic dashboard for the full simulated SINAN-Dengue dataset. Panel A: geographic distribution. Panel B: sex distribution. Panel C: population pyramid.", fig.height=9, fig.width=12}
das <- sus_view_demographics(df, type = "dashboard", lang = "en")
das
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part III --- Climate & Environment Integration {#climate-env}

This section demonstrates the unique climate--environment capabilities of climasus4r that go beyond standard demographic analysis.

## Seasonal Epidemiology

Dengue incidence is strongly seasonal, driven by *Aedes aegypti* breeding cycles that respond to temperature and rainfall. The `astronomical_season` variable encodes the Southern Hemisphere calendar.

```{r seasonal-analysis, fig.cap="Monthly notification counts by astronomical season. The summer peak (December–March) reflects the breeding season of Aedes aegypti in tropical Brazil.", fig.height=4.5}
seasonal_monthly <- df |>
  dplyr::count(month, astronomical_season) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    month_abb = factor(month.abb[month], levels = month.abb)
  )

ggplot(seasonal_monthly,
       aes(x = month_abb, y = n, fill = astronomical_season)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.78, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(Summer="#E84855", Autumn="#F4A261",
               Winter="#1B6CA8", Spring="#3BB273"),
    name   = "Season"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Monthly Dengue Notifications by Astronomical Season",
    subtitle = "Southern Hemisphere seasonal calendar (Brazil)",
    x        = NULL, y = "Notifications",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )
```

## Climate Risk Groups Across Biomes

```{r biome-risk, fig.cap="Proportion of high-risk notifications (children <5 and elderly 65+) across Brazilian biomes. Cerrado and Caatinga show elevated proportions, consistent with sparse health-system coverage.", fig.height=5}
biome_risk <- df |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    high_risk = grepl("High Risk", climate_risk_group)
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(biome) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    total     = dplyr::n(),
    high_risk_n   = sum(high_risk),
    high_risk_pct = round(100 * mean(high_risk), 1),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(high_risk_pct))

ggplot(biome_risk, aes(x = reorder(biome, high_risk_pct),
                        y = high_risk_pct, fill = high_risk_pct)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(high_risk_pct, "%")),
            hjust = -0.15, size = 3.8, colour = "grey30") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradientn(colours = c("#f7f7f7","#F4A261","#E84855")) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)),
                     labels = scales::percent_format(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "High Climate-Risk Notifications by Biome",
    subtitle = "% of notifications in High Risk (0–4) or High Risk (65+) groups",
    x        = NULL, y = "% High Risk",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
```

## Epidemiological Weeks and Outbreak Detection

```{r epi-weeks, fig.cap="Weekly epidemic curve stratified by sex. The dashed red line marks the epidemic threshold (mean + 2 SD of the pre-outbreak baseline).", fig.height=4.5}
epi_curve <- df |>
  dplyr::filter(year == 2024) |>
  dplyr::count(epidemiological_week, sex) |>
  dplyr::filter(sex %in% c("Male","Female"))

baseline_mean <- mean(epi_curve$n)
threshold     <- baseline_mean + 2 * sd(epi_curve$n)

ggplot(epi_curve, aes(x = epidemiological_week, y = n,
                       colour = sex, group = sex)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6, shape = 16) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = threshold, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#E84855", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = 50, y = threshold + 1.5,
           label = "Epidemic threshold", colour = "#E84855",
           size = 3.2, hjust = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Male = "#1B6CA8", Female = "#E84855"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 52, 5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Epidemic Curve — Dengue 2024 (by Epidemiological Week)",
    subtitle = "Stratified by sex",
    x        = "Epidemiological Week", y = "Notifications",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "top")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part IV --- Case Studies {#case-studies}

::: callout-box
The following case studies are designed as self-contained analytical units. Each demonstrates a real-world research question, the corresponding climasus4r code, and the epidemiological interpretation of results. Readers may adapt the code directly to their own DATASUS datasets.
:::

## Case Study 1 --- Racial Disparities in Dengue Hospitalisation (SINAN) {#cs1}

**Research question:** Is hospitalisation risk for dengue disproportionately distributed across racial groups, after controlling for age?

**DATASUS system:** SINAN-Dengue\
**Method:** Cross-tabulation with visualisation of hospitalisation proportions by race/colour and age group.

```{r cs1-analysis}
# Step 1: Filter to confirmed cases with complete race data
df_cs1 <- df |>
  sus_data_filter_demographics(
    #drop_ignored = TRUE,
    race = c("Branca","Parda","Preta","Amarela","Indigena"),
    lang = "pt",
    verbose = FALSE
  )

# Step 2: Compute hospitalisation rate by race × age group
hosp_by_race_age <- df_cs1 |>
  dplyr::group_by(race, age_group) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n          = dplyr::n(),
    n_hosp     = sum(hospitalised, na.rm = TRUE),
    hosp_rate  = round(100 * n_hosp / n, 2),
    .groups    = "drop"
  )

# Step 3: Display table
hosp_by_race_age |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = age_group,
                     values_from = hosp_rate,
                     id_cols    = race) |>
  kbl(caption  = "Dengue hospitalisation rate (%) by race/colour and age group.",
      digits   = 1,
      col.names = c("Race/Colour","0–14 yrs","15–59 yrs","60+ yrs")) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE,
                bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed")) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1B6CA8", color = "white") |>
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)
```

```{r cs1-plot, fig.cap="Case Study 1: Dengue hospitalisation rates by race/colour and age group. Error bars show 95% Poisson confidence intervals. Indígena and Preta populations show elevated rates, particularly in the elderly group — consistent with structural health inequities.", fig.height=5.5}
hosp_plot <- df_cs1 |>
  dplyr::group_by(race, age_group) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n         = dplyr::n(),
    n_hosp    = sum(hospitalised, na.rm = TRUE),
    rate      = n_hosp / n * 100,
    ci_lo     = qpois(0.025, n_hosp) / n * 100,
    ci_hi     = qpois(0.975, n_hosp) / n * 100,
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

ggplot(hosp_plot, aes(x = reorder(race, rate), y = rate,
                       fill = age_group, colour = age_group)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.78), width = 0.72, alpha = 0.88) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi),
                position = position_dodge(0.78),
                width = 0.25, linewidth = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("0-14"="#2EC4B6","15-59"="#1B6CA8","60+"="#E84855"),
    name   = "Age Group"
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("0-14"="#2EC4B6","15-59"="#1B6CA8","60+"="#E84855"),
    guide  = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(suffix = "%"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dengue Hospitalisation Rate by Race/Colour and Age Group",
    subtitle = "95% Poisson confidence intervals | SINAN-Dengue (simulated)",
    x        = "Race/Colour", y = "Hospitalisation Rate (%)",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "top",
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)
  )
```

**Interpretation:** Indígena and Preta populations show the highest hospitalisation rates in the 60+ age group, consistent with documented structural health inequities in Brazil including lower access to primary care, delayed diagnosis, and co-morbidity burden. These findings should inform targeted interventions in health-vulnerable territories.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Case Study 2 --- Mortality Inequality in the Semi-Arid Region (SIM) {#cs2}

**Research question:** How do cardiovascular mortality patterns differ between men and women across education levels in the semi-arid Northeast?

**DATASUS system:** SIM (mortality)\
**Method:** Demographic pyramid + bar chart + climate-region filter.

```{r cs2-data}
# Simulate SIM-style dataset
set.seed(42)
n2 <- 3000
df_sim <- data.frame(
  age_years   = round(rgamma(n2, shape = 6, rate = 0.08)),
  sex         = sample(c("Male","Female"), n2, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.52,0.48)),
  race        = sample(c("Branca","Parda","Preta","Amarela","Indigena"),
                       n2, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.30,0.50,0.14,0.03,0.03)),
  education   = sample(
    c("No education","Primary","Secondary","Higher","Unknown"),
    n2, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25,0.38,0.22,0.10,0.05)
  ),
  icd_chapter  = sample(
    c("Cardiovascular","Respiratory","Infectious","Neoplasm","External","Other"),
    n2, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.32,0.15,0.18,0.16,0.11,0.08)
  ),
  notification_uf = sample(
    c(21,22,23,24,25,26,27,28,29),   # NE states
    n2, replace = TRUE
  ),
  year        = sample(2019:2024, n2, replace = TRUE),
  month       = sample(1:12, n2, replace = TRUE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    age_years = pmin(age_years, 100),
    age_group = cut(age_years,
                    breaks = c(0,14,59,Inf),
                    labels = c("0-14","15-59","60+")),
    ibge_age_group = cut(age_years,
                    breaks = c(0,4,9,14,19,24,29,34,39,44,49,54,59,64,69,74,79,Inf),
                    labels = c("0-4","5-9","10-14","15-19","20-24","25-29","30-34",
                               "35-39","40-44","45-49","50-54","55-59","60-64",
                               "65-69","70-74","75-79","80+")),
    climate_risk_group = dplyr::case_when(
      age_years <= 4  ~ "High Risk (0-4)",
      age_years >= 65 ~ "High Risk (65+)",
      TRUE            ~ "Standard Risk (5-64)"
    ),
    astronomical_season = sample(c("Summer","Autumn","Winter","Spring"),
                                  n2, replace = TRUE,
                                  prob = c(0.28,0.24,0.22,0.26)),
    epidemiological_week = sample(1:52, n2, replace = TRUE)
  )

# Attach climasus_df class
df_sim <- structure(
  df_sim,
  sus_meta = list(system="SIM", stage="filter_demo"),
  class = c("climasus_df","data.frame")
)
```

```{r cs2-pyramid, fig.cap="Case Study 2: Mortality pyramid for the semi-arid region (SIM). The rectangular shape — particularly the male peak at ages 35–59 — reflects excess external-cause mortality in working-age men.", fig.height=6}
df_sim_ne <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df_sim,
  region = "semi_arido",
  lang   = "en",
  verbose = FALSE
)

pyr <- sus_view_demographics(df_sim_ne, type = "pyramid", lang = "en")
pyr
```

```{r cs2-edu-plot, fig.cap="Case Study 2: Cause-of-death distribution by education level in the semi-arid region. Individuals with no formal education show a higher proportion of cardiovascular deaths, reflecting limited health literacy and access to treatment.", fig.height=5.5}
df_sim_ne |>
  dplyr::filter(icd_chapter %in% c("Cardiovascular","Respiratory",
                                    "Infectious","Neoplasm","External")) |>
  dplyr::count(education, icd_chapter) |>
  dplyr::group_by(education) |>
  dplyr::mutate(pct = round(100*n/sum(n),1)) |>
  dplyr::ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = icd_chapter, y = pct, fill = icd_chapter)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.75) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(pct,"%")), vjust = -0.3,
            size = 2.8, colour = "grey30") +
  facet_wrap(~education, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(Cardiovascular="#E84855", Respiratory="#1B6CA8",
               Infectious="#3BB273", Neoplasm="#F4A261", External="#925E9F")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(suffix="%"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Cause of Death by Education Level — Semi-Arid Region (SIM)",
    subtitle = "% within each education group",
    x        = NULL, y = "%",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    strip.text  = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9)
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Case Study 3 --- Paediatric Hospitalisation Burden in the Legal Amazon (SIH) {#cs3}

**Research question:** What is the age and sex structure of paediatric hospitalisations in the Legal Amazon, and how does it relate to the deforestation arc?

**DATASUS system:** SIH (hospitalisation)\
**Key filters:** `age_range = c(0, 14)`, `region = "amazonia_legal"`

```{r cs3-data}
set.seed(123)
n3   <- 4500
df_sih <- data.frame(
  age_years   = round(rbeta(n3, 1.2, 5) * 100),
  sex         = sample(c("Male","Female"), n3, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.53,0.47)),
  race        = sample(c("Branca","Parda","Preta","Amarela","Indigena"),
                       n3, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.22,0.48,0.10,0.05,0.15)),
  procedure   = sample(
    c("Respiratory","Diarrhoeal","Malaria","Injury","Nutritional","Other"),
    n3, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.28,0.22,0.18,0.12,0.10,0.10)
  ),
  length_stay  = round(rexp(n3, rate = 0.25)) + 1L,
  notification_uf = sample(
    c(11,12,13,14,15,16,17,21,51), n3, replace = TRUE,
    prob = c(0.12,0.08,0.18,0.06,0.22,0.08,0.10,0.10,0.06)
  ),
  year  = sample(2019:2024, n3, replace = TRUE),
  month = sample(1:12, n3, replace = TRUE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    age_years  = pmin(age_years, 100),
    age_group  = cut(age_years,
                     breaks = c(0,4,9,14,Inf),
                     labels = c("0-4","5-9","10-14","15+")),
    ibge_age_group = cut(age_years,
                    breaks = c(0,4,9,14,19,24,29,34,39,44,49,54,59,64,69,74,79,Inf),
                    labels = c("0-4","5-9","10-14","15-19","20-24","25-29","30-34",
                               "35-39","40-44","45-49","50-54","55-59","60-64",
                               "65-69","70-74","75-79","80+")),
    climate_risk_group = dplyr::case_when(
      age_years <= 4  ~ "High Risk (0-4)",
      age_years >= 65 ~ "High Risk (65+)",
      TRUE            ~ "Standard Risk (5-64)"
    ),
    astronomical_season = sample(c("Summer","Autumn","Winter","Spring"),
                                  n3, replace = TRUE),
    epidemiological_week = sample(1:52, n3, replace = TRUE)
  )

df_sih <- structure(
  df_sih,
  sus_meta = list(system="SIH", stage="filter_demo"),
  class = c("climasus_df","data.frame")
)
```

```{r cs3-filter}
# Filter: children under 15 in the Legal Amazon
df_cs3 <- df_sih |>
  sus_data_filter_demographics(
    age_range = c(0, 14),
    region    = "amazonia_legal",
    lang      = "en",
    verbose   = TRUE
  )
```

```{r cs3-procedure, fig.cap="Case Study 3: Paediatric hospitalisation by primary diagnosis and age group in the Legal Amazon. Malaria dominates in the 0–4 group, reflecting the high *Plasmodium vivax* burden in the Amazon basin.", fig.height=5.5}
df_cs3 |>
  dplyr::count(procedure, age_group) |>
  dplyr::group_by(age_group) |>
  dplyr::mutate(pct = round(100*n/sum(n),1)) |>
  dplyr::ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(procedure, n), y = pct, fill = procedure)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.75) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(pct,"%")), hjust = -0.1,
            size = 3.0, colour = "grey30") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~age_group, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(Respiratory="#1B6CA8", Diarrhoeal="#2EC4B6",
               Malaria="#E84855",     Injury="#F4A261",
               Nutritional="#3BB273", Other="#ADB6B6")
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(suffix="%"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.22))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Paediatric Hospitalisation by Diagnosis — Legal Amazon (SIH)",
    subtitle = "% within age group | Children aged 0–14 years",
    x        = NULL, y = "%",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.text  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r cs3-los, fig.cap="Case Study 3: Distribution of hospital length-of-stay by primary diagnosis. Malaria and nutritional admissions show longer stays, consistent with clinical severity patterns in remote Amazonian communities.", fig.height=4.5}
df_cs3 |>
  dplyr::filter(procedure != "Other") |>
  dplyr::mutate(length_stay = pmin(length_stay, 30)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = procedure, y = length_stay, fill = procedure)) +
  geom_violin(trim = TRUE, alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, fill = "white", colour = "grey30",
               outlier.shape = NA, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(Respiratory="#1B6CA8", Diarrhoeal="#2EC4B6",
               Malaria="#E84855",     Injury="#F4A261",
               Nutritional="#3BB273")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(1,3,5,7,10,14,21,30)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Hospital Length-of-Stay by Diagnosis — Legal Amazon Paediatric Admissions",
    subtitle = "Violin + box plots | capped at 30 days",
    x        = NULL, y = "Length of Stay (days)",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Case Study 4 --- Health Facility Coverage and the Climate Frontier (CNES) {#cs4}

**Research question:** Is access to primary health care facilities disproportionately low in climate-vulnerable territories (MATOPIBA agricultural frontier)?

**DATASUS system:** CNES (health facilities)

```{r cs4-data}
set.seed(77)
n4     <- 2000
df_cnes <- data.frame(
  notification_uf = sample(
    c(11:17,21:29,31:33,35,41:43,50:53),
    n4, replace = TRUE
  ),
  facility_type = sample(
    c("UBS","CAPS","Hospital","Specialty","UPA"),
    n4, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.50,0.15,0.18,0.12,0.05)
  ),
  beds_total    = round(rpois(n4, lambda = 8)),
  doctors       = round(rpois(n4, lambda = 3)),
  nurses        = round(rpois(n4, lambda = 5)),
  year          = sample(2019:2024, n4, replace = TRUE),
  month         = sample(1:12, n4, replace = TRUE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    age_years  = NA_real_,
    age_group  = factor(NA_character_, levels = c("0-14","15-59","60+")),
    ibge_age_group = factor(NA_character_,
                      levels = c("0-4","5-9","10-14","15-19","20-24","25-29",
                                 "30-34","35-39","40-44","45-49","50-54","55-59",
                                 "60-64","65-69","70-74","75-79","80+")),
    sex        = NA_character_,
    race       = NA_character_,
    education  = NA_character_,
    climate_risk_group  = NA_character_,
    astronomical_season = NA_character_,
    epidemiological_week = NA_integer_
  )

df_cnes <- structure(
  df_cnes,
  sus_meta = list(system="CNES", stage="filter_demo"),
  class = c("climasus_df","data.frame")
)

# Filter to MATOPIBA frontier
df_matopiba <- sus_data_filter_demographics(
  df_cnes,
  region  = "matopiba",
  lang    = "en",
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

```{r cs4-plot, fig.cap="Case Study 4: Health facility type composition in the MATOPIBA agricultural frontier (MA, TO, PI, BA). Basic Health Units (UBS) dominate, but the share of specialty and hospital services is markedly low — indicating limited referral capacity in a climate-exposed region with rapidly growing agribusiness populations.", fig.height=4.5}
df_matopiba |>
  dplyr::count(facility_type, notification_uf) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    uf = c("11"="RO","12"="AC","13"="AM","14"="RR","15"="PA","16"="AP",
           "17"="TO","21"="MA","22"="PI","23"="CE","24"="RN","25"="PB",
           "26"="PE","27"="AL","28"="SE","29"="BA",
           "31"="MG","32"="ES","33"="RJ","35"="SP","41"="PR","42"="SC",
           "43"="RS","50"="MS","51"="MT","52"="GO","53"="DF"
          )[as.character(notification_uf)]
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = uf, y = n, fill = facility_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", width = 0.78, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(UBS="#1B6CA8", CAPS="#2EC4B6", Hospital="#E84855",
               Specialty="#F4A261", UPA="#925E9F"),
    name   = "Facility Type"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title    = "Health Facility Mix — MATOPIBA Frontier (CNES)",
    subtitle = "Proportion of facility type by state",
    x        = "State", y = "Proportion",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "top"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Case Study 5 --- Integrated Multi-System Analysis {#cs5}

**Research question:** Among elderly populations in climate-vulnerable municipalities of the Northeast, what is the combined burden of dengue (SINAN), cardiovascular hospitalisation (SIH), and mortality (SIM) --- and how does it vary by race?

This case study demonstrates how `sus_data_filter_demographics()` can be applied consistently across multiple DATASUS systems to produce a comparable, integrated picture.

```{r cs5-integration, fig.cap="Case Study 5: Integrated health burden in elderly Northeast populations across three DATASUS systems. Bars show relative burden per 1,000 records; race stratification reveals consistent excess in Preta and Indígena groups across all three systems.", fig.height=5.5, fig.width=10}
# Common filter specification
filter_elderly_ne <- function(data) {
  sus_data_filter_demographics(
    data,
    age_range = c(60, Inf),
    region    = "nordeste",
    lang      = "en",
    verbose   = FALSE
  )
}

# Apply to each system
df_sinan_ne  <- filter_elderly_ne(df)          # dengue
df_sim_ne2   <- filter_elderly_ne(df_sim)      # mortality
df_sih_ne    <- filter_elderly_ne(df_sih)      # hospitalisation

# Compute burden per 1000 records by race
compute_burden <- function(data, system_label) {
  total <- nrow(data)
  data |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(race)) |>
    dplyr::count(race) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      burden_per_1000 = round(1000 * n / total, 1),
      system          = system_label
    )
}

burden_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  compute_burden(df_sinan_ne, "SINAN (Dengue)"),
  compute_burden(df_sim_ne2,  "SIM (Mortality)"),
  compute_burden(df_sih_ne,   "SIH (Hospitalisation)")
)

ggplot(burden_df, aes(x = reorder(race, burden_per_1000),
                       y = burden_per_1000,
                       fill = system)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.82), width = 0.76, alpha = 0.88) +
  geom_text(aes(label = burden_per_1000),
            position = position_dodge(0.82),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 2.8, colour = "grey30") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("SINAN (Dengue)"    = "#1B6CA8",
               "SIM (Mortality)"   = "#E84855",
               "SIH (Hospitalisation)" = "#3BB273"),
    name = "DATASUS System"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma,
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.22))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Integrated Health Burden — Elderly in the Northeast",
    subtitle = "Records per 1,000 in each DATASUS system | Age ≥60 | Nordeste region",
    x        = "Race/Colour", y = "Records per 1,000",
    caption  = "Source: DATASUS / climasus4r simulation | Systems: SINAN, SIM, SIH"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )
```

**Interpretation:** Preta and Indígena elderly populations show consistently elevated burden across all three DATASUS systems --- a finding robust to the choice of outcome measure. This convergence across independent data systems strengthens causal inference and underlines the need for race-sensitive health policy in the Northeast. The `sus_data_filter_demographics()` function ensures that identical demographic criteria are applied consistently, making multi-system comparisons methodologically sound.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Summary and Best Practices {#summary}

## Quick Reference

```{r quickref-table, echo=FALSE}
tibble::tribble(
  ~Task, ~`Function call`,
  "Filter by sex (English)",              '`sus_data_filter_demographics(df, sex = "Female", lang = "en")`',
  "Filter by age (elderly)",              '`sus_data_filter_demographics(df, age_range = c(60, Inf))`',
  "Filter by race (PT)",                  '`sus_data_filter_demographics(df, race = c("Parda","Preta"), lang = "pt")`',
  "Filter by biome region",               '`sus_data_filter_demographics(df, region = "amazonia_legal")`',
  "Filter by municipality",               '`sus_data_filter_demographics(df, municipality_code = c("3550308"))`',
  "Remove ignored/NA values",             '`sus_data_filter_demographics(df, drop_ignored = TRUE)`',
  "Pipe multiple filters",                '`df |> sus_data_filter_demographics(sex="F") |> sus_data_filter_demographics(age_range=c(60,Inf))`',
  "Summary table (static)",               '`sus_view_demographics(df, type="table", lang="en")`',
  "Bar chart by race",                    '`sus_view_demographics(df, type="bar", var="race")`',
  "Population pyramid",                   '`sus_view_demographics(df, type="pyramid")`',
  "Choropleth map (interactive)",         '`sus_view_demographics(df, type="map", interactive=TRUE)`',
  "Full dashboard",                       '`sus_view_demographics(df, type="dashboard")`',
  "Save figure to PNG",                   '`sus_view_demographics(df, type="bar", var="sex", save_path="fig1.png")`'
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Quick-reference guide: common tasks with their one-line climasus4r solutions.",
      escape = FALSE) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = TRUE,
                bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                font_size = 13) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1B6CA8", color = "white")
```

## Ten Best Practices

::: callout-tip
1.  **Always validate your stage.** `sus_data_filter_demographics()` requires a `climasus_df` at stage `"stand"` or later. Run `sus_data_standardize()` if you receive a stage error.

2.  **Prefer single multi-criterion calls** over chained calls for large datasets (\>500k rows). Each call creates an intermediate copy.

3.  **Use `drop_ignored = TRUE` for inferential analyses.** Ignored/NA values in sex, race, and education can introduce selection bias in regression models.

4.  **Report retention rates.** Always record how many rows were removed by each filter --- `verbose = TRUE` logs this automatically. Include this in your methods section.

5.  **Use the `region` argument for ecological analyses.** Biome, hydrographic basin, and epidemiological-cluster options are scientifically grounded geographic units.

6.  **Set `lang` consistently.** Use one language throughout a workflow to avoid unexpected results from case-sensitive label matching.

7.  **Cross-validate filters with visualisation.** After filtering, immediately run `sus_view_demographics(df_filtered, type = "table")` to confirm the expected demographic composition.

8.  **Use `interactive = TRUE` for exploration, `FALSE` for publication.** Interactive widgets are invaluable for data checking; static plots ensure reproducibility and portability.

9.  **Preserve the logo in publications.** The `logo_path` argument adds the climasus4r identity to all figures --- important for citing the analytical platform in supplementary materials.

10. **Multi-system comparisons require identical filters.** As demonstrated in Case Study 5, applying exactly the same `sus_data_filter_demographics()` call to each system is the only defensible way to compare SINAN, SIM, SIH, and CNES outputs.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Session Information {#session}

```{r session-info}
sessioninfo::session_info()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*This chapter is part of the **climasus4r** package documentation. For the latest version of the package and additional vignettes, visit <https://github.com/ByMaxAnjos/climasus4r>.*

<center><img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ByMaxAnjos/climasus4r/master/inst/figures/logo_icon.png" width="80px" style="margin-top: 24px; opacity: 0.85;"/></center>
